title: Refinement algorithm tuning
description:
Known failure modes and candidate fixes for the Stage 3 pixel refinement
algorithm in _refine_single() / _refine_all_estimates().

## Failure modes

### 1. Oil spot / discolored ground selected over nearby orange X
Motivating case: GCP-13, DJI_20260212133226_0115_V.JPG
mean_dev + coherence multipliers added (commit 8bd1173) but no observed
improvement. Root cause likely: projected seed lands closer to oil spot
than to the X, and the oil spot is larger, so it still wins on
ratio×(λ_min+λ_max)×mean_dev×coherence.
Candidate fix: distance-from-seed soft penalty, e.g. score /= (1 + k*d).
See comment in _refine_single() near the scoring loop.

### 2. Wrong-color object selected (tree, bush) vs correct-color marker
Motivating case: GCP-96 — some images correctly find clay pigeons (orange),
others position on a nearby tree (green) or bush (grey).
Candidate fix: majority-color consensus re-evaluation pass (see below).

### 3. Asymmetric / partially-occluded X rejected by ratio test
_MIN_AXIS_RATIO = 2/3 may be too strict for real markers with one arm
partially occluded or viewed obliquely (ratio can drop to 0.3–0.5).
Candidate fix: lower threshold or make it adaptive.

### 4. High-altitude images: marker below MIN_PIXELS floor
At high AGL the marker may subtend <20 px, causing no refinement.
Candidate fix: scale MIN_PIXELS floor with estimated GSD or AGL.

### 5. Background ring contaminated by marker
If projected seed is far from actual marker, the 55–90 px background ring
may overlap the marker itself, corrupting bg_mean and suppressing
deviation_map for the correct component.

## Majority-color consensus re-evaluation (for failure mode 2)

After _refine_all_estimates() processes all (GCP, image) pairs, add a
per-GCP post-pass:

1. For each successful refinement of a GCP, record the mean LAB a*,b*
   (or HSV hue+sat) of the selected component — the "chosen color".
2. Cluster chosen colors across images for that GCP.  If a clear majority
   color exists (e.g. ≥60% of successful refinements agree on orange),
   treat that as the consensus marker color.
3. Re-run _refine_single() on:
   a. Images where refinement failed entirely.
   b. Images where the chosen component color is an outlier vs consensus
      (e.g. green or grey when most found orange).
   In the re-run, add a tight HSV/LAB color filter centered on the
   consensus color as an additional hard gate in Stage 3a, overriding the
   general anomaly detection.
4. Replace the estimate if the re-run finds a consensus-colored component.

This leverages the physical constraint that a GCP marker is the same
object in every image and should always be the same color.
notes:
## Tuning recommendations (from 85-pair ground truth comparison, 2026-03-04)

### Ground truth results
- 51% of confirmed pairs < 5 px error (sub-pixel accurate)
- 46% > 50 px error (wrong feature entirely)
- Bimodal: algorithm either nails it or completely misses, no middle ground
- Per-GCP: GCP-27 100% success, GCP-13 8% success, others mixed
- Bbox size is strong discriminator: good cases consistent, bad cases wildly variable

### Prioritized recommendations

R1. Distance-from-seed soft penalty (highest impact, simplest)
score /= (1 + k * distance_from_seed), k ~ 0.01-0.02
Encodes prior that projection estimate is usually in right neighborhood.
STATUS: implementing

R2. Bbox size consistency post-pass
Compute median bbox w/h per GCP after pass 1. Revert results whose bbox
deviates > 2x from median back to projection estimate.

R3. Minimum score threshold / fallback to projection
Do not overwrite projection with a 5x5 noise speck. Require minimum absolute
score; below threshold keep projection estimate, confidence=projection.

R4. Majority-color consensus re-pass (already designed in description)
Compute consensus marker color from successful hits, re-run failures with
tight color gate.

R5. Multi-signal post-pass (unified R2+R4)
Per-GCP: compute consensus bbox size, color, score profile. Re-run outliers
(distance, bbox, or color deviating from consensus) with tightened constraints.
This is the global vote idea from user comment.

R6. Skip refinement beyond GSD threshold
High-altitude images where marker < ~15 px are unlikely to refine successfully.
Skip refinement, keep projection estimate.

R7. Self-calibrating re-projection (speculative)
Automatically identify high-confidence (gcp,image) pairs using multiple
signals: closest to projection estimate, most nadir viewing angle, best PCA
ratio, consistent bbox size. Use these high-confidence matches as pseudo-GCPs
to compute correction factors for the drone GPS positions (effectively a
coarse bundle adjustment or affine correction per flight line). Then
re-project all low-confidence pairs using the calibrated camera positions
and re-run refinement with tighter search radii and image-stat priors
(color, bbox size) from the high-confidence matches. May be overkill but
leverages the physical constraint that GPS error is systematic/smooth, not
random per-image.

title: Auto-zoom images to GCP estimate location on load
description:
When an image loads in images-tagger and a GCP pixel estimate exists (pinLocation is set), the view should automatically pan+zoom to show the area around that GCP point. Currently images load showing the full image at 1:1 scale — GCP targets are typically tiny relative to the full image, requiring manual zoom and pan every time.

DESIRED UX:
- Image loads → immediately zoomed in and centered on the estimated GCP pixel
- Default zoom level = show ~10 sq meters of ground area (user-configurable setting)
- A '10 sq meter view' at 3cm GSD (typical drone survey) = ~333x333 pixels shown → tight enough to see the GCP target clearly

IMPLEMENTATION PLAN:

1. PIPELINE CHANGE (gcp_pipeline.py / gcp_estimates.json):
   Add GSD (ground sample distance, cm/pixel) per image to estimates JSON.
   GSD can be computed from EXIF data already available in the pipeline:
     focal_mm, focal35_mm, img_w, img_h, agl (abs_alt - site_ellip_alt_m)
     GSD_cm = (sensor_width_mm / focal_mm) * agl_m * 100 / img_w
   Include in estimates JSON per image:
     { 'px': ..., 'py': ..., 'mode': ..., 'gsd_cm': 3.2 }

2. GCPEDITORPRO CHANGE (smartimage.component.ts):
   Add @Input() autoZoomSqM: number = 10 (configurable ground area in sq meters)
   Add @Input() gsdCm: number | null = null (from estimates sidecar)
   
   After image loads (img load event) and pinLocation is set:
   a. If gsdCm is available: compute target pixel extent
        side_px = sqrt(autoZoomSqM * 10000) / gsdCm   [10000 cm² per m²]
        scale = img_w / side_px  (approximate zoom level)
   b. Else (no GSD): fall back to showing ~400px around GCP (reasonable default)
   c. Call panzoom.zoom(scale) then pan to center pinLocation in the viewport
      panzoom has zoomToPoint(scale, point) which handles both in one call

3. SETTINGS:
   Add a configurable 'autoZoomSqM' setting (localStorage persisted) with default 10.
   Display in images-tagger toolbar or a settings panel.
   Set to 0 or null to disable auto-zoom (show full image).

NOTES:
- This applies to both Standard Grid View and Zoom View right panel
- If no pinLocation (no estimate): do not auto-zoom (show full image)

title: docs: tagging guide — GCPs vs check points workflow
description:
Update docs/tagging-guide.md to explain the GCP vs check point distinction
and recommended workflow for a typical emlid2gcp.py survey.

## Sections to add / expand

### What are control points vs check points?
- Control (GCP-*): fed to ODM bundle adjustment; anchor the 3D model.
- Check (CHK-*): withheld from processing; used post-hoc to compute
  independent RMSE accuracy figures.
- Why the distinction matters: GCP residuals are optimistic (model fitted
  to them); CP residuals are the real accuracy metric.

### How accuracy (RMSE) is computed
- For each check point: dX, dY, dZ = model coordinate minus GNSS coordinate.
- RMSE_horizontal = sqrt(mean(dX²+dY²)); RMSE_vertical = sqrt(mean(dZ²)).
- Query DEM at check point GNSS location to get Z_model; query orthophoto for XY.
- Currently a manual step (QGIS or similar); ODM does not automate this yet.

### Recommended tagging workflow
Given a typical survey with ~78 surveyed points and pipeline top-7:

1. Tag top 7 GCP-* with ≥7 confirmed images each (pipeline order optimises
   spatial coverage + Z extremes + centroid anti-doming).
2. Tag 5–10 interior points as CHK-* with ≥3 confirmed images each.
   - Use the pipeline's sort order: points 8–15 are interior candidates.
   - Prefer points INSIDE the survey area, not at spatial extremes
     (extremes are already covered by GCPs).
   - Include points at varying elevations if terrain has significant relief.
3. Leave remaining ~65 points untagged (insurance against bad GCPs).
4. Do NOT use auto-refined (color_refined) estimates as check points without
   manual confirmation — pixel positions derived from the same imagery being
   processed are not fully independent.

### Exporting
- Download gcp_control.txt → feed to ODM as GCP input.
- Download gcp_check.txt  → use post-processing for RMSE calculation.
- Download gcp_list.txt   → reload / resume session.

### How many check points?
- Production mapping (ASPRS): ≥20.
- General drone survey: 5–10 is acceptable.
- Rule of thumb: ~10–15% of total surveyed points, distributed inside the
  survey area.

title: Implement GCP + image ordering in emlid2gcp.py
description:
Add structural priority sorting for GCPs and confidence sorting for images to the output of emlid2gcp.py, so that tagging in order produces optimal photogrammetric accuracy.

See docs/gcp-ordering.md for full algorithm spec.

## GCP sort (_sort_gcps)
1. Compute (mean_easting, mean_northing) centroid
2. Compute XY diagonal for scale reference
3. Z significance: z_range > z_threshold * xy_diagonal (default z_threshold=0.05)
4. Slot 1: argmax(dist_from_centroid)
5. Slot 2: argmax(dist_from_slot_1)
6. Slots 3+4: argmax/argmin(elevation) — only if Z significant
7. Next: argmin(dist_from_centroid) — center/anti-doming
8. Remaining: sorted by dist_from_centroid descending
Falls back to lat/lon/ellip_alt_m when projected coords unavailable.

## Z-critical GCP definition
Z-critical = Z is significant AND abs(elevation - mean_elev) / z_range > 0.80
These GCPs have obliques interleaved in their image ordering (see below).

## Image sort per GCP (_sort_images_for_gcp / _image_sort_score)
Sort key: score = normalized_dist + nadir_weight * (0 if nadir else 1)
- normalized_dist = sqrt((px-w/2)^2 + (py-h/2)^2) / (sqrt(w^2+h^2)/2)  [0-1]
- nadir_weight = 1.0 for normal GCPs (all nadir before oblique)
- nadir_weight = 0.3 for Z-critical GCPs (interleaves ~2-3 obliques in top 8)
- Reuses existing is_nadir() / NADIR_TOL_DEG
Falls back gracefully when img_w/img_h/gimbal_pitch absent.

## Changes required
- _sort_gcps(gcps, z_threshold) pure function — returns reordered list, tags z_critical GCPs
- _image_sort_score(px, py, img_w, img_h, gimbal_pitch, z_critical) pure function
- _sort_images_for_gcp(img_map, exif_map, z_critical) pure function
- _write_gcp_list() gains: sort_output, z_threshold, exif_map params
  Replace existing lexical/numeric sort (lines 535-539) with _sort_gcps
  Replace inner image iteration with _sort_images_for_gcp result
- run_pipeline() gains: sort_output=True, z_threshold=0.05 kwargs
  Print 'Sorting output for optimal accuracy with minimal tagging...' when sort_output=True
- CLI: --no-sort flag and --z-threshold float (default 0.05)
close_reason: Implemented and tested on ghostrider gulch (78 GCPs, 1738 images). GCP structural priority sort + per-GCP image confidence sort with z_critical interleaving. All three modes verified: default, --no-sort, --z-threshold.

title: Verify package.py-reprojected orthophoto aligns with survey points in QGIS
description:
After running package.py with --crs (via transform.yaml delivery_crs), visually
verify the reprojected orthophoto aligns correctly with known survey points in QGIS.

## Why critical
This is the first time package.py is performing CRS reprojection (via gdalwarp).
A silent misalignment (wrong source CRS assumed, axis order issue, etc.) would
produce a geometrically incorrect deliverable with no obvious error message.

## Steps
1. Open QGIS, set project CRS to EPSG:6529 (or EPSG:3618)
2. Load reprojected orthophoto output from package.py
3. Load F100340_AZTEC_points.csv (X=easting_ft, Y=northing_ft, CRS=EPSG:6529)
   or Emlid survey CSV (X=Easting, Y=Northing, CRS=EPSG:6529)
4. Confirm points fall on expected features in the orthophoto
5. Spot-check coordinates: E should be ~1,140,000–1,200,000 ft, N ~2,130,000–2,170,000 ft
   (NM Central state plane range for the Aztec site)

## Pass criteria
- Survey points land on their physical targets in the orthophoto
- No ~1 km or larger offset visible
- Coordinate display at click matches expected state-plane range

## Relevant files
- Input: ~/stratus/aztec3/odm_orthophoto/odm_orthophoto.original.tif (EPSG:32613)
- transform.yaml: ~/stratus/aztec4/transform.yaml (delivery_crs: EPSG:6529)
- Survey points: ~/stratus/aztec/F100340_AZTEC_points.csv

title: sight.py: use calibrated Brown model from previous ODM run for better initial GCP projections
description:
The Brown camera model (focal_x/y, c_x/c_y, k1/k2/k3/p1/p2) represents physical lens properties — highly stable across sites for the same camera. A calibrated model from a previous ODM run should be reusable with minimal site-to-site variation (fixed-focal drone always at infinity focus; temperature effects <0.01%).

**Problem with current sight.py**: uses EXIF focal length + assumed principal point, no distortion correction. Radial distortion (k1/k2) shifts pixels 10–30px at image edges — this is where GCPs often appear. A calibrated Brown model would improve initial pixel projections and reduce manual adjustment time in GCPEditorPro.

**Implementation**:
- Accept optional --cameras cameras.json argument in sight.py
- Parse Brown model: focal_x, focal_y, c_x, c_y, k1, k2, k3, p1, p2 (from reconstruction.json or cameras.json format)
- Apply full Brown distortion in project_3d_to_pixel() instead of simple pinhole
- Camera key format: 'v2 <make> <model> <width> <height> brown' — must match drone

**Workflow**:
- After any ODM run: pull cameras.json from S3 (e.g. ~/stratus/cameras_dji_fc6310r.json)
- Pass to sight.py: --cameras ~/stratus/cameras_dji_fc6310r.json
- Per-job cameras.json lives at s3://stratus-jrstear/{PROJECT}/cameras.json after ODM dataset stage

title: GCPEditorPro: spacebar confirms current GCP estimate in zoom view
description:
## Goal
Speed up confirmation of color-refined estimates: instead of clicking each
sub-image, the user can hover over a crop thumbnail and press Space to
confirm the estimated position in-place, then move to the next image.

## Behaviour
- Only active in zoom view (`this.zoomView === true`).
- Pressing Space while any crop thumbnail is hovered (i.e. `selectedDesc` is
  set) and `selectedDesc.isTagged` is true: confirms the estimate at its
  current pixel position (keeps `imX`/`imY` unchanged, sets `confirmed=true`
  and `confidence="confirmed"`).
- After confirming, advances `selectedDesc` to the next unconfirmed-tagged
  image in `this.images`, wrapping to the first image if none remain.
- If `selectedDesc` is null or not tagged, Space is a no-op (no error).
- Space must not fire if focus is inside a text input, number input, or
  any other form field (check `e.target.tagName`).

## Implementation — images-tagger.component.ts
- Add `@HostListener("window:keydown", ["$event"])` handler `onKeyDown(e: KeyboardEvent)`.
- Guard: `if (e.code \!== "Space") return;`
- Guard: `if (\!this.zoomView || \!this.selectedDesc?.isTagged) return;`
- Guard: `if (["INPUT","TEXTAREA","SELECT"].includes((e.target as HTMLElement).tagName)) return;`
- Call `this.pin({ x: this.selectedDesc.image.imX, y: this.selectedDesc.image.imY }, this.selectedDesc)`.
- Advance: find next image after selectedDesc in `this.images` that is not yet
  confirmed; fall back to first unconfirmed; if all confirmed keep selection.
- Call `e.preventDefault()` to prevent page scroll on Space.

## No other files need changes
- `pin()` already sets confirmed=true and confidence="confirmed" (after geo-0cu).
- No HTML changes needed.

## UX intent
The crop column in zoom view shows images in confidence order. For a well-refined
GCP, the user can work down the column rapidly: glance at crop, hover, Space,
repeat — without ever reaching for the mouse to click a precise pixel.
